summary: Gruber examines Tim Cook's 'Only Apple' mantra, arguing that Apple is indeed uniquely positioned to integrate hardware, software, and services because it controls all three with a device-centric approach. He surveys competitors — Samsung, Amazon, Google, and Microsoft — and finds each lacking a critical leg of the triad. The piece then pivots to a deeper argument: Tim Cook has quietly transformed Apple's internal operations, breaking down the fiefdom culture under Scott Forstall and enabling unprecedented cross-company collaboration. The result, visible at WWDC 2014, is an Apple that can ship major updates to both iOS and OS X simultaneously while preparing new product categories — something it couldn't do under Jobs. Gruber concludes that Apple is acting magnanimously from a position of strength, giving developers more than expected, which is more impressive than any single technical achievement.
key_points: Apple is the only company that controls devices, platforms, and services with a device-first approach, giving it integration advantages that Microsoft (platform-first) and Google (services-first) cannot match.

Tim Cook's greatest contribution has been improving Apple's internal operational efficiency, breaking down organizational silos that prevented the company from executing on multiple major initiatives simultaneously.

The October 2012 reorganization that ousted Scott Forstall was the pivotal moment — not a political reshuffling but a structural change that enabled the cross-platform collaboration on display at WWDC 2014.
Apple's scale now provides a compounding advantage: custom SoCs, Metal graphics APIs, and 64-bit ARM chips are possible because hundreds of millions of devices justify the investment.
WWDC 2014 demonstrated results that would not have been possible under Steve Jobs's leadership style, suggesting Cook is the right CEO for Apple at its current size.
money_quotes: You can copy Apple's strategy, but you can't copy their scale.
What we saw last week at WWDC 2014 would not have happened under Steve Jobs.
Jobs was a great CEO for leading Apple to become big. But Cook is a great CEO for leading Apple now that it is big, to allow the company to take advantage of its size and success.
Apple has never been more successful, powerful, or influential than it is today. They've thus never been in a better position to succumb to their worst instincts and act imperiously and capriciously.
It's downright thrilling that this is coming from Apple in a position of strength, not weakness. I'm impressed not just by what Apple can do, but by what it wants to do.

The same maestro who was able to coordinate the procurement, assembly, production, and shipment of 76 million all-new iPhones and iPads in one quarter has brought those operational instincts and unquenchable thirst for efficiency to coordinating a Cupertino.
New Apple didn't need a reset. New Apple needed to grow up.
key_insight: Tim Cook's underappreciated genius is not operational efficiency in manufacturing but in dismantling Apple's internal silos, enabling the company to leverage its integrated hardware-software-services triad at a scale and pace that was impossible under Jobs's fiefdom-tolerant leadership.
